upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a nearly saturated layer at 850 mb (1482 m MSL) with a 0°C spread, indicating the BL top is effectively capped near 1500 m (~4900 ft MSL) by a moist isothermal layer. The surface-to-850 mb lapse rate of 6.63°C/km is near moist adiabatic, suggesting modest but workable thermal potential inland once cloud cover breaks. Winds veer from ESE at surface to WNW at 850 mb (285°/11 kt) and back to SW at 700 mb (235°/11 kt), indicating some directional shear in the mid-levels but nothing severe for the sites.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area region. METARs are benign: KSFO reporting BKN/SCT040 with winds shifting from 030/7 kt to 310/10 kt — classic afternoon sea breeze onset. KHAF clear with steady NNW 10-12 kt, confirming marine push is active on the coast. KRHV reporting 300/10 kt with SCT040, backing the inland valley breeze cycle. KAPC light and variable shifting to SSW 9 kt, consistent with early afternoon valley flow. No significant anomalies.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge launch, NW-facing primary, thermal site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 ft is a thermal-dependent site where morning heating produces valley thermals that funnel up the ridge. Ideal conditions are NW winds 8-15 kt at launch with a BL top well above 2000 ft MSL. The site needs solar heating to fire thermals; cloud cover and light winds both suppress the window.
sites.todaySetup: Today's story at 1750 ft is a marginal thermal day with workable but cloud-interrupted windows. The BL climbs from ~325 m at 8 AM to a peak near 1165-1180 m (~3800-3870 ft MSL) around 1-2 PM before collapsing back, giving genuine thermal altitude in the midday window. The limiting factors are persistent cloud cover (75-100% from 10 AM through early afternoon per HRRR/GFS agreement) which will throttle thermal development, and CAPE values of 120-150 J/kg at 12-13z suggesting some instability but also potential for broken, punchy thermals under the cloud bases. Winds at launch are light NW 6-8 kt surface and 7-9 kt at 80 m, within flyable range but not robust ridge lift territory. By 5-6 PM winds pick up to 8-10 kt NW at surface and 9-12 kt at 80 m — approaching the gusty end for P2. KRHV confirms valley NW 10 kt with SCT040, consistent with the model picture.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light NW 2-7 kt with 75-90% cloud cover — thermals suppressed, BL only 325-830 m. Not a viable flying window; wait for cloud break.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL reaches 965-1180 m MSL with NW 5-7 kt at surface; best thermal window of the day if clouds thin. Heavy overcast (82-100%) may limit thermal strength — expect broken, punchy cycles rather than smooth climbs.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL collapses back to 770-815 m by 3-4 PM while surface winds build to 8-10 kt NW with 80 m winds reaching 9-12 kt. Conditions trend gustier and thermals weaken; sea breeze reinforcement arriving. P2 pilots should wrap up by 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Heavy cloud cover suppressing thermals, punchy cycles under broken overcast, afternoon wind increase to 10+ kt NW

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill launch, W/NW-facing, thermal/soaring site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is better suited for newer pilots and works on the same thermal cycle as 1750 ft but with a shallower launch altitude. It needs consistent NW 8-14 kt at launch for soaring; light or variable winds mean sled rides only.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch faces the same cloud-throttled thermal day as the upper launch, but the lower site is more sensitive to the morning direction anomaly — at 9 AM the 10 m wind briefly shifts to 250° (WSW) before returning NW, suggesting some low-level convergence or terrain channeling. The BL builds to 1045-1295 m by 11 AM-1 PM (slightly higher than 1750 ft reading, likely due to lower elevation baseline), which is encouraging, but cloud cover of 41-100% throughout the midday window limits usable thermal energy. Winds are a manageable 6-8 kt NW in the midday window, and the 80 m winds of 8-9 kt confirm usable flow. The afternoon wind spike to 10-11 kt surface / 12-13 kt at 80 m from 5-6 PM is above comfortable limits for P2 at this site. This is a suitable site for a P2 to fly if they target the narrow midday window when thermals are present but not yet too punchy.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Variable to light NW winds 3-6 kt, 41-96% cloud cover. BL building but not yet established. Sled ride territory only, not worth hiking.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: NW 6-8 kt, BL up to 1050-1295 m. Thermals possible if cloud breaks. P2 can fly here with appropriate caution for punchy cycles under the overcast.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds build to 8-11 kt NW with 80 m reaching 12-14 kt by 5-6 PM. BL collapses after 3 PM. Conditions exceed P2 comfort zone; experienced pilots only after 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Cloud-suppressed thermals, punchy cycles, afternoon wind increase, brief morning direction anomaly

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, WNW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site driven by the marine sea breeze. Ideal is WNW 12-20 kt at launch with clean laminar flow over the ridge. Too light and there's no lift band; too strong and it gets rowdy with rotor behind the ridge. The KHAF METAR and buoy winds are the primary gauges for whether the marine push has filled in.
sites.todaySetup: Today at Mussel Rock is a tale of a slow morning building into a solid afternoon — but the window is genuinely good only in the mid-to-late afternoon when sea breeze fully establishes. The morning is suppressed: winds are light and variable (ENE 2-3 kt at 8 AM, even briefly NE at 9 AM) with the marine layer not yet organized — this is confirmed by KHAF reporting NNW 10-12 kt and the SF Bar buoy at 13.6 kt NW/300°, indicating the marine push is offshore but hasn't fully filled the site gap yet. The GFS/HRRR models agree that surface winds at the site are only 2-5 kt variable from 8 AM-12 PM, well below ridge soaring minimums. The transition happens around 2-3 PM when winds clock to WSW/WNW and build to 7-9 kt, and by 3-5 PM they reach 9-11 kt WNW — a solid soaring window. The 80 m winds at 11-14 kt during peak afternoon are healthy but not excessive. SST at 14.1°C (Half Moon Bay buoy) is cool, suggesting a moderate marine layer that typically produces reliable afternoon flow once it establishes. No rotor-level concerns in the afternoon given laminar NW flow. HRRR and GFS are in excellent agreement for today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2-5 kt variable to N/NW — well below ridge soaring minimums. Not flyable; marine push not yet established at the site.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds slowly building to 5-7 kt WSW/WNW by noon, approaching but still below reliable soaring threshold. Marginal; early arrivals might catch the onset but expect lulls.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: winds build to 9-11 kt WNW by 3-5 PM with 80 m winds 12-14 kt. Classic Mussel Rock afternoon session. Flow should be consistent and laminar. Wind backs off slightly toward sunset.

verdict.hazards: Light/variable morning winds — no viable morning window. Arrive by 1:30 PM to catch the build-up.

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak launch, NW-facing, thermal site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on a combined thermal and valley breeze cycle. Ideal is NW 10-18 kt at the 2500 ft launch with a BL top above 3500 ft MSL. The site is punchy and rowdy in strong conditions and requires confident thermalling skills. Sea breeze typically arrives from the SW in the afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak today mirrors the Ed Levin story but with some additional complexity from being a higher, more exposed launch. The BL tops out at a decent 1165-1180 m MSL (~3800-3870 ft MSL above sea level) at 1-2 PM, which is above launch altitude and should provide legitimate thermal altitude for confident P3 pilots. The major limiting factor is persistent cloud cover — 75-100% from 10 AM through mid-afternoon — which will suppress thermal development significantly and create the classic 'punchy under broken overcast' scenario. Winds at launch are light-moderate NW 5-8 kt in the midday window, acceptable but not robust. The 120 m wind layer (representing flow well above launch) shows a building NW gradient reaching 10-14 kt by mid-to-late afternoon, signaling stronger flow aloft. KRHV 300/10 kt and KAPC SSW 9 kt both confirm valley circulation is established. The evening wind spike to 10 kt surface / 12-14 kt at 80 m with CAPE falling to near zero signals the thermal window closes by 4-5 PM and sea breeze conditions take over.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NW 2-7 kt, 44-89% cloud cover, BL only 325-830 m — below launch altitude much of the morning. Not flyable; thermals not established.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best thermal window: BL reaches 965-1180 m with NW 5-7 kt. Expect thermal cycles under patchy overcast — flyable for P3 but expect turbulence. CAPE 80-150 J/kg adds some texture.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: BL collapses after 2 PM, wind builds to 8-10 kt NW with 120 m layer reaching 10-14 kt — increasingly turbulent sea breeze conditions. P3 pilots should be on final glide by 4 PM.

verdict.hazards: Heavy overcast suppressing thermals, punchy cycles under cloud base, CAPE-driven instability, afternoon wind increase

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks cloudier and weaker. GFS/HRRR both show 100% cloud cover through the morning and midday with BL topping near 470-495 m — effectively capped. Winds are WNW 8-9 kt consistent but the combination of full overcast and shallow BL means no usable thermal window. Tomorrow.io adds low visibility (2-5 km) in the afternoon with a suspicious cloud/fog layer. Likely NO FLY for thermal flying.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same story as 1750 ft — full overcast all day with BL capped below 600 m for most of the day. Tomorrow.io shows visibility dropping to 2-5 km in the afternoon. This is a sled-ride-only day at best. NO FLY for meaningful soaring.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks strong at Mussel Rock. GFS/HRRR agree on 11-13 kt NW surface winds from the morning (8 AM onward) with 80 m winds 14-17 kt. Tomorrow.io shows gusts 16-19 kt all day — strong but potentially on the upper edge for comfortable P2 flying. 100% cloud cover in the morning may signal marine layer overhead but winds are robust. Best window likely midday to afternoon when clouds partially break. P3+ conditions.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks poor thermally — full overcast all day per GFS/HRRR, BL capped near 385-495 m, and Tomorrow.io shows visibility dropping to 1.5-3.6 km with 77-100% cloud cover. Winds are WNW 8 kt but with no thermal development possible under this marine influence. NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday (Apr 15) shows persistent 100% cloud cover all day per GFS, with BL reaching 900-1130 m by afternoon — better than Tuesday but still overcast. Winds NW 6-9 kt. If the marine layer breaks, there could be a late thermal window, but confidence is low. MARGINAL at best.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Similar to 1750 ft for Wednesday — full overcast but BL building to 1035-1130 m. Winds NW 7-9 kt. A late afternoon break could allow soaring but the persistent 100% cloud cover in the GFS data suggests the marine layer is firmly entrenched. MARGINAL/NO FLY.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday shows continued strong NW flow at Mussel Rock — 13-15 kt surface, 80 m winds 13-17 kt. This is a solid ridge soaring day if you can handle the strength. 100% cloud cover through most of the day but ridge sites don't need sun. Good P3 day.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday remains completely overcast with BL reaching 960-1100 m but clouds persisting all day. Winds shift more toward WNW/W at 270-280° and 80 m winds build to 10-14 kt — not ideal direction for the launch. MARGINAL to NO FLY.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Day 3 (Apr 16) is beyond reliable model range but the pattern suggests continued marine influence. Monitor for any break in the onshore flow regime.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same pattern as upper launch for Day 3 — marine layer persistence is the dominant forecast question. Watch for model disagreement as confidence drops beyond 48 hours.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: The consistent NW flow pattern through Day 3 suggests Mussel Rock remains the most reliable flying site of the week. Strong onshore gradient should maintain afternoon ridge soaring opportunities.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 forecast confidence is low. Current pattern of marine-influenced overcast and W/WNW winds doesn't favor good thermal days. Check updated models Wednesday evening.

watchlist: Marine layer persistence: buoy SST at 14.1°C (cool) and 100% cloud cover forecast Tue-Wed suggests the marine layer is deeply entrenched — thermal sites may be suppressed for multiple days
Mussel Rock Tuesday gusts: Tomorrow.io forecasts 16-19 kt gusts all day — strong for P2 pilots, verify conditions on arrival before committing
Ed Levin afternoon wind spike: today's 5-6 PM window sees surface winds reach 10 kt NW with 80 m winds 12-14 kt — don't linger past 4 PM
Afternoon visibility drops at Ed Levin and Mission Peak Tuesday (Tomorrow.io shows 1.5-5 km) — possible marine layer intrusion into the valley
tldr: Today's best bet is Mussel Rock in the afternoon — wait until 2 PM for the sea breeze to fill in properly and enjoy a classic 9-11 kt WNW ridge session through sunset. Inland thermal sites (Ed Levin, Mission Peak) are flyable but cloud-throttled with a narrow midday window for P3 pilots willing to deal with punchy cycles under the overcast. The rest of the week looks increasingly marine-dominated — Mussel Rock stays good while thermal sites go soft.
